message.content: Yesterday I created a depot in this project that has the unusual hicpu setting, and I did a cpu quota increase to 96 cores manually.  Can you determine if that is the depot currently selected by RBRR?

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
#!/bin/bash
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Repository Configuration - Base Values

# Resource prefixing — both required, 2–11 chars, lowercase, ending in hyphen (e.g., "prod-", "bhyslop-").
# Placeholder values below carry a visible "change-me" hint via the 'x' marker.
RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX=cancbhl-
RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX=canrbhl-

RBRR_DNS_SERVER=8.8.8.8

# Reference directory containing specs for all vessels: relative to project root
RBRR_VESSEL_DIR=rbev-vessels

# Container workspace path — working directory for all bottle containers
RBRR_BOTTLE_WORKSPACE=/workspace

# Depot configuration — moniker pairs with CLOUD_PREFIX to derive
# project ID, GAR repository, pool stem, and bucket at kindle.
RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=canest2bhl100011
RBRR_GCP_REGION=us-central1

# Google Cloud Build settings
RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32
RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT=2700s
RBRR_GCB_MIN_CONCURRENT_BUILDS=3

# Secrets directory — contains all credential files (RBRA and RBRO format)
# Credential file paths are derived as kindle constants in rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh
RBRR_SECRETS_DIR=../station-files/secrets

# Public documentation URL — target for buh_tlt glossary links.
# Update per-release (swap branch name) or per-incorporation (point at your fork).
RBRR_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL="https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md"

# eof

lastPrompt: Yesterday I created a depot in this project that has the unusual hicpu setting, and I did a cpu quota increase to 96 cores manually.  Can you determine if that is the depot currently selected by RBRR?

aiTitle: Check if RBRR uses high-CPU depot

content.text:
Yes — the depot currently selected by RBRR is the one you provisioned yesterday.

**`.rbk/rbrr.env`** (lines 35, 39):
- `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=canest2bhl100011`
- `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32` (the unusual hicpu setting)

The git trail from 2026-05-15 confirms it's the same depot:
- `4fe8f500` (₢BOAAA): "increment moniker to **canest2bhl100011** for fresh depot levy under the **32-cpu gauntlet shakedown**"
- `a7a98cd8`: marshal-zero defaults `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` to `e2-highcpu-32` so the setting survives regime resets
- `7564c9d9` (₢BOAAD wrap): "**quota approval to 96 cores** landed mid-pace … confirming the approved quota carries production-shape conjures against **cancbhl-d-canest2bhl100011**"

So RBRR is pointing at the same depot that received the 96-core quota approval.

message.content: what was the commit that started the experiment to use the larger cloud machine?

content.text:
**`ba3b7b3d`** — `jjb:1011-5b847a7e:₣BL:n: bump RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE to e2-highcpu-32 for informal performance trial — supersedes the formal A/B bench scope of ₢BLAAA with a single-axis high-bound run; relevy required for take-effect, quota check advised before`

Affiliated with heat ₣BL. Note the "relevy required for take-effect, quota check advised before" — that's the commit that set up the conditions you then satisfied yesterday with the new depot levy and 96-core quota bump.

file.content:
# Theurge-own colophons (routed by rbte_dispatch, not the RBK zipper)
  readonly ZRBTE_COLOPHONS="rbtd-ap"

  # Combined manifest: RBK zipper colophons + theurge-own colophons
  readonly ZRBTE_FULL_MANIFEST="${ZRBZ_COLOPHON_MANIFEST} ${ZRBTE_COLOPHONS}"

  # Suite-to-fixture mappings
  ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "handbook-render"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
  )
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

rbte_build() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Building theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  buc_success "Theurge built"
}

rbte_test() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  buc_step "Testing theurge"
  buc_log_args "Manifest: ${RBTE_MANIFEST}"
  cargo test --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" "$@" || buc_die "cargo test failed"
  buc_success "All theurge tests passed"
}

rbte_run() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_fixture="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_fixture}" || buc_die "No fixture imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh)"

  zrbte_build_binary

  buc_step "Running theurge fixture '${z_fixture}'"
  "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" "${ZRBTE_FULL_MANIFEST}" "${z_fixture}"
}

rbte_suite() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  local z_suite="${BURD_TOKEN_3:-}"
  test -n "${z_suite}" || buc_die "No suite imprint — use tabtarget with imprint (e.g. rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh)"

input.description: Map onboarding-sequence case structure

lastPrompt: I realize the 'gauntlet' suite cannot run due to the fact taht the default created depot only has 2 cpus; what tests can we run using the existing depot that are in gauntlet?

aiTitle: Check if RBRR uses high-CPU depot

content.text:
From `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh:63-76`, the gauntlet is these 12 fixtures. Two of them levy their own depot — they're the ones that can't run against the existing `canest2bhl100011`:

**Cannot run** (re-levy a depot, defeating the 96-core quota you got):
- `pristine-lifecycle` — case 1 is the marshal-zero entry-contract gate; later cases stand up a throwaway depot via `rbtdrp_depot_stand_up` then tear it down (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrp_pristine.rs:673,1161`). Refuses to run with a non-blank moniker in `rbrr.env` anyway.
- `canonical-establish` — case 1 is `rbtdrk_depot_levy` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs:407`); picks the next free `canest…` suffix and levies a fresh default-quota depot. This is the fixture that originally minted `canest2bhl100011`; re-running it would mint `…100012`.

**Can run** on the existing depot (10 of 12, and the crucible/cloud-build ones actually exercise the 96-core quota):
- `enrollment-validation` — state-indifferent (per the comment at `rbte_engine.sh:60`)
- `onboarding-sequence` — no `depot_levy`/`depot_unmake` in `rbtdro_onboarding.rs`; inscribes reliquary + ordains conjures against the current depot
- `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `dockerfile-hygiene` — fast-tier, no GCP
- `hallmark-lifecycle` — uses existing depot's GAR
- `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml` — crucible fixtures, depot-agnostic

You can drive these directly with `tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.{name}.sh` one at a time, or via the existing `crucible`/`service` suites (which already cover 8 of the 10) plus the three extras (`dockerfile-hygiene`, `onboarding-sequence`, `moriah`) run individually.

content: You're verifying the 96-core depot canest2bhl100011 is still selected by RBRR and figuring out which gauntlet fixtures can run against it. Next: run the 10 safe fixtures individually, skipping pristine-lifecycle and canonical-establish. (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
Disk survey (threshold 85%): 228 GB: 72%
Heat: rbk-99-performance-study (₣BL)
Status: stabled
Created: 260513


## Paces

### cloudbuild-machinetype-ab-bench (₢BLAAA) [rough]

Drafted from ₢A_AAZ in ₣A_.

## Character

Curiosity-driven benchmark, hand-orchestrated. Goal is data, not a
binding decision — no production config changes by default. Single
deliverable: a memo with findings and a recommendation (follow-up
heat, itch, or close).

## Motivation

Build wall-clock has drifted out of memory. Want a number on whether
bigger CPU helps conjure, or whether the pipeline is network/IO-bound.

Slating investigation surfaced the load-bearing constraint:
`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` is a *depot-time* setting baked into private
worker pools at levy. Both pools (tether and airgap) inherit it.
Conjure submits to the egress-mode-matched pool; vouch is hardwired to
the airgap pool. No per-build `options.machineType` override path
exists. Each machineType condition therefore requires its own depot
lifecycle (or, at minimum, pool teardown-and-recreate — no tabtarget
for that today).

This structural fact is independently useful — capturing it in the
output memo earns the pace its keep even if the timing data is
inconclusive.

## What

Vessel under test: moriah's bottle (`rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`).
Heavier than `rbev-busybox`, conjure-mode, multi-platform Rust binary
— enough real build work to surface CPU-vs-IO signal. Sentry vessel
out of scope. Tadmor no longer viable (moved to bind/graft).

Two depot lifecycles, one per machineType condition:
  - Condition A: current default `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` (record the
    value at start of pace).
  - Condition B: upgraded Compute Engine type — `e2-standard-8`,
    `n2d-standard-8`, or `c3-standard-8`. Pick at mount-time.
  - Optional Condition C: further-upgraded value if B shows clear
    scaling worth extending the curve.

## How — per condition

  1. Edit `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` in `rbrr.env` to the target value.
     Temporary commit on a throwaway branch — do not merge to main.
  2. Tear down existing depot (`rbw-dU`); levy fresh (`rbw-dL`). The
     airgap and tether pools come up with the new machineType.
  3. Mantle governor (`rbw-aM`), invest retriever (`rbw-arI`), invest
     director (`rbw-adI`). The IAM-propagation race on AR-resource-scope
     getIamPolicy is captured in
     `Memos/memo-20260513-iam-propagation-race-director-invest-gar.md`;
     divest + re-invest is the known recovery if director invest fails
     ~30s in.
  4. Inscribe reliquary (`rbw-dI`); yoke into moriah (`rbw-dY`).
  5. Enshrine moriah's bottle base (`rbw-dE rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`).
  6. Ordain bottle 3× back-to-back via
     `rbw-fO rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`. Discard run 1 as worker-pool /
     registry-cache warmup; measure runs 2 and 3.
  7. Run vouch once (`rbw-fV`) over the resulting hallmarks. Time it
     separately — vouch is a distinct Cloud Build on the same airgap
     pool, may parallelize differently than conjure.
  8. Capture per-build wall-clock from `gcloud builds list` and
     per-step durations from build logs. Note any anomalies (cache
     misses, stragglers, propagation retries). Record raw data in a
     per-condition section of the memo.

## Outcome

Memo at `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-cloudbuild-machinetype-bench.md` containing:
  - The structural finding: machineType is pool-time, not build-time;
    private pools vs default-shared-pool distinction; consequence for
    any future tuning effort.
  - Per-condition: machineType value, per-build wall-clock, per-step
    durations, anomalies.
  - Verdict: CPU-bound, IO-bound, mixed; identify where the bottleneck
    sits in the conjure/vouch pipeline (base pull, layer build, layer
    push, SBOM, multi-arch assembly, etc.).
  - Recommendation: bump default `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` (and to
    what), nominate a follow-up heat, itch it, or close the question.

## Constraints

  - No production-config changes from this pace. The `rbrr.env` edit
    lives on a throwaway branch and is reverted before close.
  - GAR cleanup not needed — an imminent operator-scheduled depot
    rebuild subsumes anything left behind.
  - About-pipeline (RBSAB) runs as steps inside conjure on the same
    pool; its share of the wall-clock is captured by per-step
    durations, not analyzed separately.

  3. Mantle governor (`rbw-aM`), invest retriever (`rbw-arI`), invest
     director (`rbw-adI`). The IAM-propagation race on AR-resource-scope
     getIamPolicy is captured in
     `Memos/memo-20260513-iam-propagation-race-director-invest-gar.md`;
     divest + re-invest is the known recovery if director invest fails
     ~30s in.
  4. Inscribe reliquary (`rbw-dI`); yoke into moriah (`rbw-dY`).
  5. Enshrine moriah's bottle base (`rbw-dE rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`).
  6. Ordain bottle 3× back-to-back via
     `rbw-fO rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`. Discard run 1 as worker-pool /
     registry-cache warmup; measure runs 2 and 3.
  7. Run vouch once (`rbw-fV`) over the resulting hallmarks. Time it
     separately — vouch is a distinct Cloud Build on the same airgap
     pool, may parallelize differently than conjure.
  8. Capture per-build wall-clock from `gcloud builds list` and
     per-step durations from build logs. Note any anomalies (cache
     misses, stragglers, propagation retries). Record raw data in a
     per-condition section of the memo.

## Outcome

Memo at `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-cloudbuild-machinetype-bench.md` containing:
  - The structural finding: machineType is pool-time, not build-time;
    private pools vs default-shared-pool distinction; consequence for
    any future tuning effort.
  - Per-condition: machineType value, per-build wall-clock, per-step
    durations, anomalies.
  - Verdict: CPU-bound, IO-bound, mixed; identify where the bottleneck
    sits in the conjure/vouch pipeline (base pull, layer build, layer
    push, SBOM, multi-arch assembly, etc.).
  - Recommendation: bump default `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` (and to
    what), nominate a follow-up heat, itch it, or close the question.

## Constraints

  - No production-config changes from this pace. The `rbrr.env` edit
    lives on a throwaway branch and is reverted before close.
  - GAR cleanup not needed — an imminent operator-scheduled depot
    rebuild subsumes anything left behind.
  - About-pipeline (RBSAB) runs as steps inside conjure on the same
    pool; its share of the wall-clock is captured by per-step
    durations, not analyzed separately.

File-touch bitmap: (no work file changes)

Two paces touch this question:

**₣BL rbk-99-performance-study** (stabled)
- **₢BLAAA cloudbuild-machinetype-ab-bench** [rough] — the formal A/B benchmark. Docket already names the raison d'être: "want a number on whether bigger CPU helps conjure, or whether the pipeline is network/IO-bound." Two depot lifecycles, ordain moriah 3× per condition, vouch once, capture wall-clock + per-step durations into a memo. Structural finding (machineType is pool-time, not build-time) is preserved as standalone value.

**₣BO rbk-11-mvp-tactical** (racing)
- **₢BOAAA gauntlet-32cpu-shakedown** [rough] — supposed to run the full gauntlet under the 32-cpu tweak. The commit `ba3b7b3d` that bumped the machine type explicitly said it "supersedes the formal A/B bench scope of ₢BLAAA with a single-axis high-bound run" — i.e., this pace was the informal substitute for ₢BLAAA.

The natural recast: with `canest2bhl100011` already provisioned at 96-core quota + `e2-highcpu-32` setting, you've effectively *already paid for* Condition B of ₢BLAAA. What's missing is the actual measurement and a comparison point. Two reasonable shapes:

1. **Recast ₢BLAAA in place** — drop the two-lifecycle requirement; measure conjure+vouch on the current depot, compare against historical numbers (build logs from prior `e2-medium` runs already in GCP). Retire ₢BOAAA as subsumed.
2. **Recast ₢BOAAA** — narrow from "run gauntlet" to "measure moriah conjure+vouch on this depot," and keep ₢BLAAA stabled in ₣BL for a future proper A/B.

Which heat do you want the recast to live in?

(1) Port mapping was wrong: said the directive value was 'unchanged' (7999:7999), but without sentry's PREROUTING DNAT translating 7999→8000(/8080), the publish itself must translate. Corrected to asymmetric form `"${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}"` — for srjcl `7999:8000`, for pluml `7999:8080`. (2) Added new Step 3: lift `ip_forward=1` enablement out of the entry-port block in rbjs_sentry.sh to an unconditional earlier line, so kernel-forward state is independent of which access-mode iptables block runs. The current entry-port block at line 121-122 enables ip_forward as a side-effect; deletion leaves pluml (access_mode=disabled) with ip_forward=0 because pluml's access-mode block doesn't touch ip_forward. Lifting normalizes kernel state across access modes; functional behavior unchanged (pluml outbound remains iptables-blocked). (3) Expanded hallmark refresh from 'srjcl and pluml' to 'every nameplate exercised in the test iteration' — sentry vessel is shared across srjcl/pluml/tadmor/moriah/ccyolo, so any unrefreshed nameplate in the iteration runs against the OLD sentry image and its pass/fail validates the old image rather than the new one. For the three-fixture iteration (srjcl + pluml + tadmor), refresh all three. (4) Split verification into iteration-cycle (three-fixture, early signal) and canonical-cycle (full pristine gauntlet on both platforms, declaration gate). Memo principle and topology reframe conclusion unchanged; this is housekeeping on the experiment protocol following coding-agent triage.
9eb7588d jjb:1011-28d1411f:₢A_AAP:n: Capture the srjcl jupyter-connectivity regression diagnosis surfaced by the AAP gauntlet acceptance run. The memo records: symptom (HTTP 000 from curl localhost:7999/lab against pristine-conjured srjcl crucible); root cause (Docker port-publishes via alphabetically-first attached network, which is the enclave — packets arrive on eth0, but rbjs_sentry.sh:124-126 scopes the PREROUTING DNAT to eth1 via ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}, so DNAT matches 0 packets); lineage (₢BBABC commit 6d836814 renamed sentry's services.networks.default → transit which removed the load-bearing 'default' magic that made compose pick the right gateway and port-publish target by accident; ₢BBABE commit ed898867 caught and fixed the default-route half of this same fragility, but the port-publish half was missed); cross-platform empirical confirmation (linux Docker engine 28.x and macOS Docker Desktop 28.x, both against branch tip 28d1411f); macOS-specific second-order blocker (after relaxing the DNAT scope, RBM-FORWARD's eth1-ingress/eth0-egress ACCEPT still drops the hairpinned post-DNAT path — linux's parallel investigation reported HTTP 200 from DNAT-half alone but the macOS evidence shows the FORWARD half is empirically necessary too); fix shape (sentry-side, both halves, drop ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} from both PREROUTING DNAT at line 125 and RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT at line 129; consistent with BBABE's 'sentry takes ownership of its networking' precedent; requires sentry vessel re-kludge + nameplate hallmark refresh on srjcl and pluml since the sentry image is shared); sibling risk (pluml shares the sentry image and entry-mode-enabled posture — same bug will surface there); files involved (rbjs_sentry.sh lines 124-130, rbob_compose.yml lines 32-37, rbtdrc_crucible.rs line 1620); verification gate (rbtdrc_srjcl_jupyter_connectivity green via full pristine gauntlet on BOTH platforms, non-negotiable). The memo is structured to be self-contained for a clean-chat mount in a separate pace — it points at sources rather than restating them where possible, but captures enough empirical detail (per-interface PREROUTING log counters, both platforms' DNAT outputs, FORWARD chain dump) to support implementation without re-probing the running crucibles.
28d1411f jjb:1011-ecff6d9b:₢A_AAP:n: Bump srjcl's RBRN_BOTTLE_READINESS_DELAY_SEC from 30 to 120. Surfaced during the AAP gauntlet acceptance run: pristine-lifecycle reached srjcl after 54/54 tadmor cases passed, but rbtdrc_srjcl_jupyter_connectivity got HTTP 000 (curl couldn't establish TCP) against a freshly-conjured Jupyter image. Re-running the fixture in isolation reproduced HTTP 000 every time. The jupyter process was up inside the bottle (ps aux confirmed at +1min into the container's lifetime, bound to 0.0.0.0:8000), and the 30s readiness delay did execute per the rbob_charge log line 'Waiting 30s for bottle service readiness' — but a freshly-conjured Jupyter image takes longer than 30s to bind its HTTP listener. Triage class: grammar — the value introduced in ₢BBAAt (commit 0a850d57) was calibrated against cached/warm Jupyter images and is insufficient for the pristine-cutover path the gauntlet exercises. 120s is the operator-chosen value from the 60/90/120 menu, providing strong margin for the cold-conjure path. RBRN_BOTTLE_READINESS_DELAY_SEC survives marshal-zero (which only blanks hallmark fields), so this value persists across the next depot regen cycle. The charge tabtarget refused to proceed with the file uncommitted ('Nameplate has uncommitted changes: .rbk/srjcl/rbrn.env — commit before charging'), forcing this commit before fixture re-run. Pluml carries the same 30s value and will need a parallel bump if its connectivity case fails the same way later in the gauntlet.
667ef2cf jjb:1011-a637fc06:₢A_AAP:n: Ignore .claude/scheduled_tasks.lock — runtime artifact from Claude Code's ScheduleWakeup machinery, parallel to the existing .claude/settings.local.json and .claude/settings.json entries. Surfaced when rblm_zero's working-tree-clean precheck refused to proceed at the start of the AAP gauntlet acceptance run because the file was untracked.
bfd52c82 jjb:1011-44837042:₢BBAAy:W: Collapsed rbtdre_Section into a flat cases: &[Case] field on rbtdre_Fixture. The Section struct, its name field, the rbtdre_run_sections function, and the per-section header printout are gone; rbtdre_run_cases now iterates one ordered list and emits only per-case PASSED/FAILED/SKIPPED. Step 1 (drop kebab from user-facing case output) folded into Step 2 — the only kebab surface in case output was section names, which vanish with the Section struct; case!() macro already emitted function names. Every fixture-defining module (rbtdrf_fast, rbtdrf_handbook, rbtdrp_pristine, rbtdro_onboarding, rbtdrk_canonical, rbtdrl_calibrant, rbtdrc_crucible) replaced its RBTDR*_SECTIONS_* statics with flat RBTDR*_CASES_* arrays preserving declaration order across the former section boundaries. calibrant-fail-fast lost the artificial 2-section split: 4 cases → 3 by dropping rbtdrl_failfast_not_reached_inter (equivalent to _intra once sections vanish); diagnostic needle shifted from 'intra-section' to 'fail trigger'. Engine self-tests in rbtdte_engine.rs dropped two now-redundant section-specific tests (fail_fast_stops_across_sections, multiple_sections_run_sequentially) with the ordering invariant preserved as rbtdte_cases_run_in_declaration_order; rbtdte_zero_sections + rbtdte_section_with_zero_cases collapsed to rbtdte_zero_cases. Test-side renames *_sections_registered → *_cases_registered across rbtdtk_canonical/rbtdto_onboarding/rbtdtl_calibrant, reading fix.cases directly. main.rs single-case dispatch now reads fixture_def.cases. colors.white disappeared with the section header. Theurge-ifrit context doc retitled 'Test Sections' → 'Test Cases', section table converted to purpose-grouping bullets, 'Adding a New Test' guidance points at RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY. Verified: tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh clean, tt/rbtd-t.Test.sh 94/94 pass, tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh 98/98 across enrollment-validation/regime-validation/regime-smoke/handbook-render with operator output now showing pure function names (rbtdrf_ev_string_valid etc.). Crucible fixtures (tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) and credentialed fixtures (hallmark-lifecycle, batch-vouch, access-probe, pristine-lifecycle, canonical-establish, onboarding-sequence) compile and pass unit-level case_registered tests, but not exercised at runtime in this pace — that surfaces under BBAAX's full rbw-tP run. Notch landed at 44837042 with size_limit=80000 (72,118 bytes, mechanically-large refactor touching 14 files with no surprise bulk).
10497fb4 jjb:1011-3a3ad5f2:₣A_:T: aak-acceptance-tP-macos-linux
1d10105a jjb:1011-9f39fcdd:₢BBABB:W: BURS_TINCTURE per-station tincture landed under heat-affiliated commit b2ffca63 (deferred from pace-affiliation pending BBABA collision-awareness reslate, which has since landed). Verification on this officium: theurge unit suite 97/97 (including both wrap-criterion tests rbtdtp_pristine_dual_station_disjoint and rbtdtk_canonical_dual_station_disjoint covering disjoint project_id, GAR repo, GCS bucket, and SA email across two distinct tinctures with 30-char GCP project-ID cap assertion); fast suite 98/98 across 4 fixtures. Mount-time open question resolved: BURS_TINCTURE is required (1-3 char lowercase alphanumeric leading-letter, validated at burs_regime.sh:42,66-67). Production code unchanged per docket — only burs_regime.sh, BUS0, rbrr_regime.sh comment-only validator update, and theurge fixture composers/tests touched. BBABA's docket already references BBABB landing as a satisfied cross-pace dependency; the parallel-station property is now first-class for BBABA's dual-machine rbw-tP execution.
7124cec7 jjb:1011-66aa5e5d:₣A-:n: Add §Supported Platforms qualifying release-1 against native Docker on Linux, Docker Desktop on Windows (WSL2 backend with per-distro integration, single-daemon), and Docker Desktop on Mac (Apple Virtualization or Docker VMM). Aligns with RBS0 §at_runtime stance (Docker today, Podman deferred); points readers at §Future Work for the Podman accommodation. Linux qualification verified by inspecting cerebro (Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS, kernel 6.8.0-107, Docker 28.1.1, cgroup v2/systemd) currently running rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet.sh — the release-gate suite consumed by ₣BB rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification. Inserted as H3 inside §Environment after the two-capability summary so the qualification appears before any setup-flow mentions; complements paddock ₣A- §Standing Notes Docker Desktop entry landed in commit 66aa5e5d.
b2ffca63 jjb:1011-1ae8b6c2:₣BB:n: Add BURS_TINCTURE per-station tincture (1-3 char lowercase alphanumeric, leading letter, no hyphen, required) to BURS schema; pristine and canonical theurge fixtures compose it into both the throwaway/canonical RBRR cloud and runtime prefixes AND the family stem used for moniker construction, so parallel rbw-tP runs from different stations land in disjoint depot project IDs, GAR repos, GCS buckets, and SA emails on a shared payor manor. Fixture-side mechanism: const RBTDRP_THROWAWAY_CLOUD_PREFIX='prlc-' (and runtime/canonical/family-stem siblings) split into _BASE consts (no tincture, no trailing hyphen) plus composer fns rbtdr{p,k}_{throwaway,canonical}_{cloud,runtime}_prefix(t) / rbtdr{p,k}_family_stem{,_arc}(t); install/probe/moniker-pick sites call the composer with tincture read from BURD_STATION_FILE via rbtdr{p,k}_burs_tincture() (parses station-file BURS_TINCTURE; BURS validation upstream guarantees format). Director/Retriever SA names left unchanged per docket out-of-scope — their emails inherit project-id disjointness once depots disjoint, so 'pristl-ret' and 'canest-ret' SA names are stable across stations. RBRR joint-length validator updated comment-only (no arithmetic change) acknowledging tincture composition behavior; the validator does NOT cross-reference BURS_TINCTURE because BURS isn't kindled in every RBRR-enforce context (rbrr_cli furnish doesn't source burs_regime), so reaching ${BURS_TINCTURE} would trip set -u. Fixture bases are short (≤7 chars: prlc/prlr/canc/canr=4, pristl=6, canest2=7) so tinctured composed values (≤25-26 chars) stay well under the 30-char GCP project-ID cap. Verification: 5 new Rust unit tests covering composer shape, per-tincture disjointness, and dual-station dry-run of full RBDC composition (mirrors rbdc_DerivedConstants.sh:84-87 inline) for project_id, GAR repo, bucket, SA email — all pass; theurge unit suite green at 97/97; fast suite green at 98/98 (regression). BUS0 spec gains :burs_tincture: linked term with axtu_string motif and constraint description. Station file (../station-files/bur

content.content:
1	BURN_HOST=rocket
2	BURN_PLATFORM=bubep_windows
3

1	# Pristine gauntlet failure — IAM read-after-create flap on `rbtdrp_sa_cycle`
2	
3	**Date**: 2026-05-14
4	**Run start**: 2026-05-14 17:00:17 local
5	**Invocation**: `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` (gauntlet test suite)
6	
7	## Branch state at run time
8	
9	- Branch: `cerebro-BB-002`
10	- HEAD: `d545c7661d1cf7a03bc5ac4c17efd23bcc09e655` — *pristine-lifecycle fixture: set RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=pristlbhl100009*
11	- Merge-base against `main`: `5838194f4aed46f1d385866a33dd8cc53337394d` — *jjb:1011-ae38563d::i: OFFICIUM 260513-1040*
12	- Commits ahead of `main` (3):
13	  - `d545c7661` pristine-lifecycle fixture: set RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=pristlbhl100009
14	  - `db1ff74f2` pristine-lifecycle fixture: install throwaway RBRR prefixes (prlcbhl-/prlrbhl-)
15	  - `cd93504d7` Marshal Zero — release qualification reset
16	- Working tree at run time: clean.
17	
18	## Test outcome
19	
20	`tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` → `rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet` → fixtures run:
21	
22	| Fixture | Cases | Result |
23	|---|---|---|
24	| enrollment-validation | 47 | 47 passed |
25	| pristine-lifecycle | 3 | 2 passed, 1 **failed** |
26	
27	Failed case: **`rbtdrp_sa_cycle`** (the third pristine-lifecycle case). Marshal-zero attestation and depot stand-up both passed before it.
28	
29	## Forensic artifacts
30	
31	- Top-of-tree logs (host filesystem):
32	  - `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260514-170017-1358543-227.txt` (QualifyPristine)
33	  - `../logs-buk/hist-rbtd-s-gauntlet-20260514-170017-1358579-122.txt` (gauntlet driver)
34	  - `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-arI-sh-20260514-170249-1364416-563.txt` (the failing `GovernorInvestsRetriever` invocation)
35	- Theurge trace dir: `/tmp/rbtd-1359516/`
36	  - `rbtdrp_sa_cycle/trace.txt` — `FAILED: rbtdrp_sa_cycle / invest retriever exit 1`
37	  - `rbtdrp_sa_cycle/moniker.txt` — `pristlbhl100009`
38	  - `rbtdrp_sa_cycle/governor-sa-email.txt` — `governor-202605141702@prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009.iam.gserviceaccount.com`
39	  - `rbtdrp_sa_cycle/invest-retriever-{stdout,stderr}.txt` (stderr empty)
40	  - `burv/invoke-00004/` — the BURD invocation that wrapped the failing tabtarget
41	    - `output/current/burx.env`: `BURX_EXIT_STATUS=1`, began 17:02:49, ended 17:02:52 (3 s wall)
42	    - `temp/temp-20260514-170249-1364416-563/transcript.txt` — full call trace
43	
44	## Precise failure point (from transcript)
45	
46	Identity in play:
47	- Depot project: `prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009`
48	- Retriever SA being invested: `retriever-pristl-ret@prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009.iam.gserviceaccount.com` (uid `104464931844201861787`)
49	
50	Sequence of HTTP calls inside `rbgg_invest_retriever` → `zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key`:
51	
52	| # | Call | Endpoint | Result |
53	|---|---|---|---|
54	| 1 | Create SA | `POST iam.googleapis.com/v1/projects/.../serviceAccounts` | **200** (uid returned) |
55	| 2 | SA propagation probe (by email) | `GET .../serviceAccounts/{email}` | **200** — `"SA propagation (by email) ready after 0 seconds"` |
56	| 3 | Keys subresource propagation probe | `GET .../serviceAccounts/{email}/keys` | **200** — `"Keys subresource propagation ready after 0 seconds"` |
57	| 4 | Real List keys | `GET .../serviceAccounts/{email}/keys` | **404** — `Service account ... does not exist.` → `buc_die` |
58	
59	So the read-after-create view at Google's IAM front door went **200 → 200 → 404** within microseconds. The existing `rbgu_poll_until_ok` helper accepted the first 200 as "ready," but IAM's visibility was still flapping non-monotonically. That makes this *not* a "missing propagation wait" — it's a "propagation wait accepts a single transient 200" bug.
60	
61	stderr is empty; the failure surfaces only via stdout because the path through `rbgu_http_require_ok` → `buc_die` writes its error line to the standard log channel.
62	
63	## Locale where a fix could go
64	
65	Two candidate sites, narrow → broad:
66	
67	1. **Narrow (symptom-local)** — `Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh:198-208`, the Preflight block immediately after the `Keys subresource propagation` probe. Wrap the `List keys` call (`rbgu_http_json` + `rbgu_http_require_ok`) in a small retry/back-off so a single transient 404 here doesn't abort the whole investment. Fixes this specific failure but doesn't help anywhere else the same pattern bites.
68	
69	2. **Structural (root-cause)** — `Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh:570-596`, the `rbgu_poll_until_ok` helper. It returns ready on the *first* 200 (`if test "${z_code}" = "200"; then ... return 0`). Make readiness require **N consecutive 200s** (double-tap, or even triple-tap) against IAM's flapping window — same helper, same callers, but now resistant to the observed `200 → 200 → 404` pattern. This is the higher-leverage fix; `rbgu_poll_until_ok` is the propagation primitive used wherever the codebase says "wait until this Google resource is visible," so hardening it benefits every caller.
70	
71	Existing related concept doc: `RBSCIP-IamPropagation.adoc` (governs how the codebase treats IAM propagation). Worth re-reading before picking option 2, since the helper change is a contract change for every caller.
72	
73	## Reproducibility note
74	
75	This run was against a freshly-minted pristine depot (`prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009`, governor minted at `202605141702`). Pristine runs create a brand-new GCP project per cycle, which is exactly the regime that exposes wide IAM propagation windows. A naive re-run is *likely* to pass; the bug is in the helper's tolerance of IAM's view, not in this branch's logic.

file.content:
# Pristine gauntlet failure — IAM read-after-create flap on `rbtdrp_sa_cycle`

**Date**: 2026-05-14
**Run start**: 2026-05-14 17:00:17 local
**Invocation**: `tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` (gauntlet test suite)

## Branch state at run time

- Branch: `cerebro-BB-002`
- HEAD: `d545c7661d1cf7a03bc5ac4c17efd23bcc09e655` — *pristine-lifecycle fixture: set RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=pristlbhl100009*
- Merge-base against `main`: `5838194f4aed46f1d385866a33dd8cc53337394d` — *jjb:1011-ae38563d::i: OFFICIUM 260513-1040*
- Commits ahead of `main` (3):
  - `d545c7661` pristine-lifecycle fixture: set RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=pristlbhl100009
  - `db1ff74f2` pristine-lifecycle fixture: install throwaway RBRR prefixes (prlcbhl-/prlrbhl-)
  - `cd93504d7` Marshal Zero — release qualification reset
- Working tree at run time: clean.

## Test outcome

`tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh` → `rbtd-s.TestSuite.gauntlet` → fixtures run:

| Fixture | Cases | Result |
|---|---|---|
| enrollment-validation | 47 | 47 passed |
| pristine-lifecycle | 3 | 2 passed, 1 **failed** |

Failed case: **`rbtdrp_sa_cycle`** (the third pristine-lifecycle case). Marshal-zero attestation and depot stand-up both passed before it.

## Forensic artifacts

- Top-of-tree logs (host filesystem):
  - `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260514-170017-1358543-227.txt` (QualifyPristine)
  - `../logs-buk/hist-rbtd-s-gauntlet-20260514-170017-1358579-122.txt` (gauntlet driver)
  - `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-arI-sh-20260514-170249-1364416-563.txt` (the failing `GovernorInvestsRetriever` invocation)
- Theurge trace dir: `/tmp/rbtd-1359516/`
  - `rbtdrp_sa_cycle/trace.txt` — `FAILED: rbtdrp_sa_cycle / invest retriever exit 1`
  - `rbtdrp_sa_cycle/moniker.txt` — `pristlbhl100009`
  - `rbtdrp_sa_cycle/governor-sa-email.txt` — `governor-202605141702@prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009.iam.gserviceaccount.com`
  - `rbtdrp_sa_cycle/invest-retriever-{stdout,stderr}.txt` (stderr empty)
  - `burv/invoke-00004/` — the BURD invocation that wrapped the failing tabtarget
    - `output/current/burx.env`: `BURX_EXIT_STATUS=1`, began 17:02:49, ended 17:02:52 (3 s wall)
    - `temp/temp-20260514-170249-1364416-563/transcript.txt` — full call trace

## Precise failure point (from transcript)

Identity in play:
- Depot project: `prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009`
- Retriever SA being invested: `retriever-pristl-ret@prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009.iam.gserviceaccount.com` (uid `104464931844201861787`)

Sequence of HTTP calls inside `rbgg_invest_retriever` → `zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key`:

| # | Call | Endpoint | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create SA | `POST iam.googleapis.com/v1/projects/.../serviceAccounts` | **200** (uid returned) |
| 2 | SA propagation probe (by email) | `GET .../serviceAccounts/{email}` | **200** — `"SA propagation (by email) ready after 0 seconds"` |
| 3 | Keys subresource propagation probe | `GET .../serviceAccounts/{email}/keys` | **200** — `"Keys subresource propagation ready after 0 seconds"` |
| 4 | Real List keys | `GET .../serviceAccounts/{email}/keys` | **404** — `Service account ... does not exist.` → `buc_die` |

So the read-after-create view at Google's IAM front door went **200 → 200 → 404** within microseconds. The existing `rbgu_poll_until_ok` helper accepted the first 200 as "ready," but IAM's visibility was still flapping non-monotonically. That makes this *not* a "missing propagation wait" — it's a "propagation wait accepts a single transient 200" bug.

stderr is empty; the failure surfaces only via stdout because the path through `rbgu_http_require_ok` → `buc_die` writes its error line to the standard log channel.

## Locale where a fix could go

Two candidate sites, narrow → broad:

1. **Narrow (symptom-local)** — `Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh:198-208`, the Preflight block immediately after the `Keys subresource propagation` probe. Wrap the `List keys` call (`rbgu_http_json` + `rbgu_http_require_ok`) in a small retry/back-off so a single transient 404 here doesn't abort the whole investment. Fixes this specific failure but doesn't help anywhere else the same pattern bites.

2. **Structural (root-cause)** — `Tools/rbk/rbgu_Utility.sh:570-596`, the `rbgu_poll_until_ok` helper. It returns ready on the *first* 200 (`if test "${z_code}" = "200"; then ... return 0`). Make readiness require **N consecutive 200s** (double-tap, or even triple-tap) against IAM's flapping window — same helper, same callers, but now resistant to the observed `200 → 200 → 404` pattern. This is the higher-leverage fix; `rbgu_poll_until_ok` is the propagation primitive used wherever the codebase says "wait until this Google resource is visible," so hardening it benefits every caller.

Existing related concept doc: `RBSCIP-IamPropagation.adoc` (governs how the codebase treats IAM propagation). Worth re-reading before picking option 2, since the helper change is a contract change for every caller.

## Reproducibility note

This run was against a freshly-minted pristine depot (`prlcbhl-d-pristlbhl100009`, governor minted at `202605141702`). Pristine runs create a brand-new GCP project per cycle, which is exactly the regime that exposes wide IAM propagation windows. A naive re-run is *likely* to pass; the bug is in the helper's tolerance of IAM's view, not in this branch's logic.

content.text:
Found it.

**Historical successful gauntlet run** — commit `0ee5726f` (2026-05-14 22:17 UTC):
- 236 cases, 12 fixtures, all passed
- Suite ran 2026-05-14 20:31 → 22:00 UTC (1h 29m)
- HEAD `e93c78336`
- **Log**: `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260514-203134-1368806-985.txt`

The log is **not on this Mac** — the PID `1368806` is far too large for darwin and matches the failed-run logs from the same day's memo (`Memos/memo-20260514-pristine-gauntlet-iam-flap.md`, PIDs in the 1.3M range). Both runs were on the `rocket` Windows fundus (`BURN_HOST=rocket`, `BURN_PLATFORM=bubep_windows` per `.buk/rbmn_nodes/bujn-winpc/burn.env`).

**Two paths to mine substep durations**:

1. **Foray to rocket**: bind a legatio to `rocket`, `jjx_fetch` the log file, then parse it locally. Most direct — gets us the exact tabtarget-level breakdown the gauntlet recorded.

2. **Mine Cloud Build directly via `gcloud builds list`** against the May 14 historic depot (`pristlbhl100009` for pristine-lifecycle, then whatever `canonical-establish` minted for the conjure/vouch builds). Cloud Build retains build records with per-step timings well past 2 days. This bypasses the log file and gets us actual GCB step durations — which is what we need for the CPU-vs-IO question anyway, since pristine-lifecycle teardown/setup time isn't useful for that comparison.

For the raison d'être (does bigger CPU make conjures faster?), path 2 is actually better than path 1 — the gauntlet log captures wall-clock per fixture, but `gcloud builds list` gets per-step durations inside each conjure/vouch build. That's where CPU vs IO signal lives.

Which path do you want to start with? Or run both — foray for the gauntlet structure, gcloud for the build internals?

You're right — I missed two. Apologies for the wrong call.

**Two clean local gauntlet runs on macOS:**

| Log | Date | Duration | Fixtures |
|-----|------|----------|----------|
| `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260506-110454-14392-84.txt` | May 6 11:04 → 12:42 | 1h 38m | 11 (pre-dockerfile-hygiene) |
| `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260512-125848-3104-247.txt` | May 12 12:58 → 14:38 | 1h 40m | 11 (pre-dockerfile-hygiene) |

Both end with `Suite 'gauntlet' complete (11 fixtures)` — 0 failures. PIDs in normal macOS range (14392, 3104), trace dirs under `/var/folders/h1/...` confirming local execution.

Note: the May 14 remote run had 12 fixtures; the local ones predate the `dockerfile-hygiene` addition to the gauntlet (e6e2a2e4 minted that fixture mid-May). For substep-duration mining, that's a minor difference — dockerfile-hygiene is fast-tier (no Cloud Build), so its absence doesn't distort the CPU-vs-IO signal we care about.

**May 12** is the better baseline — closer to current architecture, same depot/SA flow as today. Want me to parse it now for per-fixture and per-build wall-clock? I can produce a substep timing table you can drop straight into a memo or into the recast ₢BOAAA docket.

aiTitle: Check if RBRR uses high-CPU depot

content.text:
Found two more on cerebro, including the May 14 success.

**Cerebro full-pass gauntlet logs**:

| Log | Date (local) | Duration | Fixtures | Notes |
|-----|--------------|----------|----------|-------|
| `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260512-201330-1167187-166.txt` | May 12 20:13 → 21:49 | 1h 36m | 11 | pre-dockerfile-hygiene |
| `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260514-203134-1368806-985.txt` | May 14 20:31 → 22:10 | **1h 39m** | **12** | the run referenced in commit `0ee5726f` |

Cerebro is in fact the fundus that ran the May 14 success (not rocket — I was wrong about that earlier; `/tmp/rbtd-...` and Linux-shaped PIDs confirm cerebro).

**Full baseline inventory now**:
- macOS local: May 6 (11 fix), May 12 (11 fix)
- cerebro: May 12 evening (11 fix), May 14 evening (12 fix) — the 12-fixture run is the highest-fidelity baseline since it includes `dockerfile-hygiene` (matches today's gauntlet shape)

The May 14 cerebro log is the strongest reference for the recast: closest in time to today's architecture, full 12-fixture coverage, ran on a Linux host with no IAM flap (clean pass).

Want me to pull the May 14 cerebro log down and parse it for per-fixture and per-Cloud-Build substep timings? That gives you the baseline numbers for the recast ₢BOAAA — then we measure the corresponding builds today on `canest2bhl100011` (`e2-highcpu-32`) and the comparison memo writes itself.

message.content: Not yet.  Lets design new pace docket.  I think you can mine these logs to get an expected duration for each build process.  We'll need to do that, then run builds using highcpu (i.e. current depot) 'single case run' and then we can compare.  Help me decide whether we do all the builds or just some of them?  Lets not do work yet, we are planning new pace docket..

content.text:
Designing the docket — let's think through what's worth measuring.

## What Cloud Builds the gauntlet actually exercises

From `rbtdro_onboarding.rs` (and parallel cases in canonical-establish and pristine-lifecycle), the gauntlet hits these distinct GCB workloads, all on the depot's `private-default` (tether) or `private-airgap` pool:

| Workload | Operation | Shape | What it stresses |
|----------|-----------|-------|------------------|
| Reliquary inscribe | `rbw-dI` | Multi-image parallel mirror | Registry I/O, very little build CPU |
| Enshrine base | `rbw-dE` | Single-image mirror | Registry I/O |
| Conjure sentry | `rbw-fO` (small) | Single-arch, thin Dockerfile | Light build CPU, mostly base+push |
| Conjure bottle (moriah/jupyter) | `rbw-fO` (medium) | Single-arch, Rust/Python deps | Real build CPU + layer push |
| Bind plantuml | `rbw-fO bind` | Multi-arch + SBOM + digest pin | Heaviest — multi-arch assembly, syft scan |
| Graft demo | `rbw-fO graft` | Local push only | None on Cloud Build |
| Vouch | `rbw-fV` | Verification chain | Distinct CB, similar profile across runs |

## The decision dimensions

**1. Which workloads to include?** Three reasonable shapes:

- **Maximalist** (all six CB workloads above): full surface, ~1h, lets us see whether CPU helps unevenly across build types (the most interesting answer). But noise across runs hides small differences.
- **Targeted** (moriah conjure + plantuml bind + vouch, 3× each): the three workloads where CPU could most plausibly matter — Rust/Python compile, multi-arch SBOM, and verification. ~30-40 min. Matches the original ₢BLAAA "heavy vessel" instinct.
- **Minimal** (moriah conjure 3×): tightest signal on one workload. ~15 min. Sacrifices breadth but cheapest to re-run if the depot state needs reset.

**2. Repetitions per workload?**
Original ₢BLAAA said "3× back-to-back, discard run 1 as warmup." That's defensible for moriah conjure but expensive for plantuml bind (10-20 min each). Tradeoff: 3× moriah + 1× plantuml + 1× vouch may be the right asymmetry.

**3. The comparison baseline problem**
We can't run a same-day default-CPU control — the depot is on `e2-highcpu-32`. The historical gauntlet logs are our only baseline, but they sit on cerebro (Linux fundus) while today's runs come from this Mac. Two ways to handle this:

- **Per-step ratio comparison**: pull substep durations from both, look at *ratios* between CB-step types (network pull vs build vs push). Robust to host noise.
- **Wall-clock-only comparison**: simpler, but conflates depot-CPU with host-CPU. Less defensible.

The structural finding in the original ₢BLAAA ("machineType is pool-time, not per-build") already earns the memo its keep regardless of measurement quality — so even noisy data is acceptable as long as we're honest about it.

**4. The `gcloud builds list` opportunity**
For any build that ran in the gauntlet, GCB retains per-step `startTime`/`endTime` for ~30+ days. We can mine the May 14 cerebro run's per-step durations *directly from GCB* without parsing the log file. Same for today's runs. That bypasses most fundus/host noise entirely.

## My recommendation

**Targeted** (3 workloads: moriah conjure 3×, plantuml bind 1×, vouch 1×) with **substep timing pulled from `gcloud builds list`** rather than log scraping. Reasons:

- Moriah is what the original ₢BLAAA picked, and is the most representative of real-world "I'm waiting for a build" UX
- Plantuml bind catches the multi-arch / SBOM upper bound
- Vouch is short enough to include for free, and it's the build that always runs on the airgap pool regardless of vessel mode
- GCB substep durations sidestep the host/fundus comparison problem
- ~30-40 min total wall clock — fits within a single session

Skip reliquary inscribe and enshrine — they're I/O-dominated, won't move with CPU, and you've already measured them implicitly during yesterday's levy (the wrap commit captured "reliquary inscribe in 3m11s" and "busybox conjure in 1m33s").

Does this shape match what you want, or do you want broader/narrower?

input.content:
# jjezs_reslate BOAAA

## Character

Measurement pace. Hand-orchestrated; goal is data plus a memo
recommendation. Single deliverable. Skips the full gauntlet because
the current depot's 96-core quota cannot be re-levied at default
2-cpu without forfeiting it.

## Motivation

`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32` is currently in effect on the
canest depot. The original A/B benchmark in ₣BL's stabled pace
₢BLAAA called for two depot lifecycles; that structural path is
blocked by the quota fact. This pace instead measures the current
depot's per-build substep durations against a historic 12-fixture
clean-gauntlet baseline. The structural finding from ₢BLAAA
(machineType is pool-time, not build-time) carries over and earns
the memo its keep regardless of measurement noise.

## What — three Cloud Build workloads, single-case run via tabtargets

1. moriah bottle conjure (`rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`), 3× back-to-back
   — discard run 1 as worker-pool warmup; runs 2 and 3 are the data.
2. plantuml bind (`rbev-bottle-plantuml`), 1× — multi-arch + SBOM
   upper bound.
3. vouch (`rbw-fV`), 1× — airgap-pool verification chain.

Skip reliquary inscribe and enshrine — registry-I/O dominated, won't
move with CPU. Yesterday's levy already exercised one inscribe and
one busybox conjure on this depot; their durations fold into the
memo as side data.

## How

Depot stays as-is — no rbrr.env edits, no relevy. Drive each workload
directly via its tabtarget. Capture wall-clock from the tabtarget log
and per-step duration via `gcloud builds list` + `gcloud builds
describe` for both the current runs and the historic baseline.

Baseline: the May 14 cerebro clean-pass log
`hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260514-203134-1368806-985.txt` (12 fixtures, 1h 39m)
plus the corresponding GCB records on cerebro's e2-standard-2 depot
(retained 30+ days). Two macOS-local 11-fixture clean passes (May 6
and May 12) are available for cross-host sanity; the May 14 cerebro
run is the canonical reference.

## Outcome

Memo at `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-cloudbuild-machinetype-bench.md` carrying
the structural finding from ₢BLAAA's docket plus:
  - Per-workload comparison table — 32cpu vs 2cpu, decomposed by GCB
    step where the build's `steps[]` permits.
  - Verdict: CPU-bound, IO-bound, or mixed — per workload, not summed.
  - Recommendation: keep `e2-highcpu-32` as default, roll back to
    `e2-standard-2`, or pick a middle value.

## Constraints

  - No production-config changes from this pace. Rollback (or non-
    rollback) lives in the follow-up pace.
  - Comparison is cross-host (mac vs cerebro fundus). Per-step
    *ratios* between build phases are more defensible than absolute
    deltas; the memo states this caveat.
  - 96-core quota stays intact — no depot levy/unmake.

# jjezs_slate machine-type-restore-e2-standard-2

## Character

Mechanical config rollback. Decision posture from the bench pace's
memo recommendation — keep highcpu, roll back, or pick a middle value.

## What

Two locations carry the experiment's `e2-highcpu-32` setting:

  - `.rbk/rbrr.env` operational value (commit ba3b7b3d installed
    the bump)
  - marshal-zero default in the rblm CLI, raised together so
    regime resets preserved the experiment (commit a7a98cd8
    establishes the wiring location)

Both revert together to whatever the bench memo lands on.

## Constraints

  - Take-effect on rbrr.env requires re-levy of the depot. The
    current depot carries a 96-core quota; a relevy at default
    2-cpu forfeits it. Operator decides whether the re-levy
    happens under this pace or is deferred.
  - Skip entirely if the bench memo recommends keeping highcpu.

content.text:
Yes — ₢BOAAA is next actionable (position 2, rough; position 1 ₢BOAAD is complete).

One refinement worth catching now while context is hot: I wrote a "cross-host caveat" into the constraints, but on reflection it's misleading. GCB per-step durations come from GCB's own metadata (`gcloud builds describe` returns server-recorded `startTime`/`endTime` per step) — they're independent of the host that submitted the build. The cerebro-vs-mac concern only applies to the tabtarget *wall-clock totals* (orchestration: git ops, gcloud RPCs, polling). The CPU-vs-IO signal we care about lives in the GCB substep numbers, which are cleanly comparable across hosts because the pool's machineType is the only thing that differs.

Two other things I noticed but think are correctly left to mount-time judgment:

- moriah is an **airgap** nameplate — its ordain triggers the forge → airgap chain (multi-build), not a single conjure. "3× back-to-back" is heavier than for a single-conjure vessel. Mount agent can decide whether to keep the 3× or reduce to 2×.
- `rbw-fV` vouches *all* hallmarks at once, not a single one. Mount agent can decide whether to time the full sweep or scope down via filter.

Want me to tighten the docket's cross-host constraint before you mount? Quick edit — I'd rewrite that bullet to say the caveat applies only to tabtarget wall-clock totals, and that GCB substep durations are host-independent.

diff --git a/.rbk/rbrr.env b/.rbk/rbrr.env
index 90a67d7c..db772900 100644
--- a/.rbk/rbrr.env
+++ b/.rbk/rbrr.env
@@ -19,8 +19,8 @@
 
 # Resource prefixing — both required, 2–11 chars, lowercase, ending in hyphen (e.g., "prod-", "bhyslop-").
 # Placeholder values below carry a visible "change-me" hint via the 'x' marker.
-RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX=
-RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX=
+RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX=prlcbhl-
+RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX=prlrbhl-
 
 RBRR_DNS_SERVER=8.8.8.8
 
@@ -32,7 +32,7 @@ RBRR_BOTTLE_WORKSPACE=/workspace
 
 # Depot configuration — moniker pairs with CLOUD_PREFIX to derive
 # project ID, GAR repository, pool stem, and bucket at kindle.
-RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=
+RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=pristlbhl100011
 RBRR_GCP_REGION=us-central1
 
 # Google Cloud Build settings

 # Depot configuration — moniker pairs with CLOUD_PREFIX to derive
 # project ID, GAR repository, pool stem, and bucket at kindle.
-RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=
+RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=pristlbhl100011
 RBRR_GCP_REGION=us-central1
 
 # Google Cloud Build settings

input.content:
# jjezs_reslate BOAAA

## Character

Measurement pace. Hand-orchestrated; goal is a defensible, replication-
backed conclusion plus a memo recommendation. Solidity over speed —
cost is acceptable, weak data is not.

## Motivation

`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32` is currently in effect on the
canest depot (canest2bhl100011 carries a 96-core quota approval).
The original A/B benchmark in ₣BL's stabled pace ₢BLAAA called for
two depot lifecycles; that structural path is blocked because the
existing quota cannot be re-levied at default 2-cpu without
forfeiting it. This pace measures the current depot's per-build
substep durations against historic clean-gauntlet baselines whose
depots ran at `e2-standard-2`. The structural finding from ₢BLAAA
(machineType is pool-time, not build-time) carries over and earns
the memo its keep regardless of measurement noise.

## Pre-conditions

A pristine-lifecycle fixture run subsequent to ₢BOAAA's slating
overwrote `.rbk/rbrr.env` to throwaway state (final commit on the
overwrite chain: 0225aa6a, prlcbhl-/prlrbhl- prefixes,
RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=pristlbhl100011). The 96-core canest depot
itself was not touched on GCP — only the local config.

Before any bench operation:
  1. Restore canest operational state in `.rbk/rbrr.env` — recover
     cancbhl-/canrbhl- prefixes and RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=canest2bhl100011
     from commit 4fe8f500.
  2. Verify the depot is alive via `tt/rbw-dl.PayorListsDepots.sh`.
  3. Verify `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32` is still set.

## What — three Cloud Build workloads, single-case run via tabtargets

Replication targets are 1 warmup + N data per workload; warmup runs
are discarded (worker-pool cold-cache, registry-cache cold).

1. moriah bottle ordain (`rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`), 1 warmup + 3 data
   = 4 runs. Airgap nameplate — ordain triggers forge→airgap chain
   (multi-build); wall-clock cost is correspondingly higher than a
   single conjure but exercises both the tether pool (forge) and
   airgap pool (airgap conjure) in one workload.
2. plantuml bind (`rbev-bottle-plantuml`), 1 warmup + 2 data = 3 runs.
   Multi-arch + SBOM upper bound, runs on tether pool.
3. Vouch, 1 warmup + 2 data = 3 runs. Scope each vouch to a single
   hallmark rather than letting `rbw-fV` sweep all hallmarks — the
   sweep's wall-clock conflates per-hallmark cost. Use moriah and
   plantuml hallmarks produced above as the vouch targets, rotating.
   Vouch is hardwired to the airgap pool regardless of vessel mode.

Skip reliquary inscribe and enshrine — registry-I/O dominated, won't
move with CPU. Yesterday's levy already exercised one inscribe and
one busybox conjure on this depot; their durations fold into the
memo as side data.

## How — data capture

For every Cloud Build triggered (including warmup discards):
  - Capture `gcloud builds describe BUILD_ID --format=json` — the
    full record preserves per-step `startTime`/`endTime`,
    `options.machineType`, queue time, pool routing, and step
    `status`. This is the substrate the memo builds on.
  - Capture the tabtarget's host log alongside (orchestration
    overhead is secondary signal but trivial to keep).
  - Per-build artifacts live under `Memos/bench-data-YYYYMMDD/`
    as `<workload>-run-N.json` plus a manifest noting build ID,
    timestamp, expected vs actual machineType, pool, and any
    anomalies. The memo references the directory; raw data
    survives independently of the memo's analysis choices.

If a build fails mid-bench: capture the failed build's JSON anyway
(failure mode is data) and replace that run rather than silently
retrying. The memo must be honest about run-count.

## How — baseline mining

Four historic clean-pass runs are available, all on e2-standard-2:

| Date | Host | Fixtures | Log |
|------|------|----------|-----|
| May 14 | cerebro | 12 | `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260514-203134-1368806-985.txt` |
| May 12 | cerebro | 11 | `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260512-201330-1167187-166.txt` |
| May 12 | mac local | 11 | `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260512-125848-3104-247.txt` |
| May 6 | mac local | 11 | `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260506-110454-14392-84.txt` |

May 14 cerebro is the canonical reference (matches today's 12-fixture
shape including dockerfile-hygiene). The other three are
cross-checks. Pull the build IDs each gauntlet run produced for the
three target workloads (moriah airgap chain, plantuml bind, vouch)
from each log, then `gcloud builds describe` them — the records are
retained for 30+ days. Variance across the four historic runs sets
the noise floor against which today's highcpu deltas are judged.

## Outcome

Memo at `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-cloudbuild-machinetype-bench.md`
carrying:
  - The structural finding from ₢BLAAA (machineType is pool-time,
    not build-time; consequence for any future tuning effort).
  - Per-workload comparison table — 32cpu (today, data runs only)
    vs 2cpu (four historic baselines). Decompose by GCB step where
    the build's `steps[]` permits; show mean and spread across
    replications on both sides.
  - Per-step ratios alongside absolute deltas. Ratios are robust to
    queue-time and registry-cache variance; deltas tell the
    operational story.
  - Noise-floor section: across the four historic 2cpu baselines,
    what is the natural step-by-step variance? Today's deltas must
    clear that floor to count as signal.
  - Verdict per workload, not summed: CPU-bound (proportional
    speedup), IO-bound (no movement), or mixed (which step types
    move). The same machineType bump may yield different verdicts
    for forge vs airgap conjure vs bind vs vouch.
  - Recommendation: keep `e2-highcpu-32` as default, roll back to
    `e2-standard-2`, or pick a middle value. The recommendation
    must cite per-workload numbers, not gestalt.

## Constraints

  - No production-config changes from this pace. Rollback (or non-
    rollback) lives in the cantle pace.
  - GCB substep durations are host-independent — server-recorded by
    GCB, retrieved by `gcloud builds describe`. Tabtarget wall-clock
    totals carry host-dependent orchestration overhead (git ops,
    gcloud RPCs, polling); the memo distinguishes the two.
  - 96-core quota stays intact — no depot levy/unmake operations.
  - rbrr.env restoration to canest config is a working-tree edit;
    do not commit it to main — the throwaway state is the
    last-fixture artifact and the next pristine-lifecycle run will
    overwrite again. Stash or branch as needed.

content.text:
Reslate landed. Summary of the substantive changes:

- **Pre-conditions section added** — surfaces the rbrr.env throwaway-state hazard from the just-pulled fixture commits, with the recovery recipe (cancbhl-/canrbhl- + canest2bhl100011 from commit 4fe8f500). Avoids the mount agent walking into a dead config.
- **Replications expanded** — moriah 3→4 runs (1 warmup + 3 data), plantuml 1→3 (1 warmup + 2 data), vouch 1→3 (1 warmup + 2 data), all data runs analyzed independently.
- **Vouch scoped** — single hallmark per run rather than `rbw-fV` sweep, so a single vouch's timing is isolable.
- **Data capture tightened** — full `gcloud builds describe --format=json` per build (including warmups and failures), persisted under `Memos/bench-data-YYYYMMDD/` independently of the memo's analysis. Raw data survives.
- **Baseline broadened** — all four historic clean passes (May 6 mac, May 12 mac, May 12 cerebro, May 14 cerebro) mined for variance. The May 14 cerebro stays canonical but the other three set the noise floor.
- **Cross-host caveat corrected** — moved into the right scope: applies to tabtarget wall-clock totals, not to GCB substep durations which are host-independent.
- **Memo gains a noise-floor section** — today's deltas must clear baseline variance to count as signal.
- **rbrr.env discipline note** — keep restoration as working-tree edit, don't commit, since the next pristine run will overwrite anyway.

You're clear to mount in a fresh chat — ₢BOAAA highcpu-build-substep-bench is next actionable in ₣BO.

message.content: Whoops!  I didn't realize I caused that but indeed I did.  Okay, is that cleanup properly docketed?  Or should we do that restoring of RBRR pointing to 32 cpu depot now?

content.text:
It's docketed — but as a pre-condition for the mount agent to handle, not as a discrete step. Worth questioning that choice now that you've raised it.

The "don't commit, working-tree edit only" rule I wrote into the docket constraint was over-cautious on reflection. The argument against committing was "the next pristine-lifecycle run will overwrite anyway" — but that cuts both ways: main *normally* carries whatever state the last operation left it in, and committing canest config is the established pattern (commit `4fe8f500` did exactly that after a similar fixture-pollution event). Keeping main's rbrr.env reflective of the operational depot is the cleaner discipline.

Three options:

1. **Restore + commit now** — clean main state going into mount; eliminates one setup step from the bench pace; matches the `4fe8f500` precedent. Commit affiliates to ₣BO as `S` (or to ₢BOAAA — but it's not really bench *work*, it's housekeeping). I'd also strip the "don't commit" constraint from the docket since it would be moot.
2. **Restore as working-tree edit only** — defer commit decision to mount agent. Riskier (edit can get lost across sessions).
3. **Leave for mount agent** — as docketed. They do the restore as part of their pre-condition checks.

My recommendation: **option 1**, heat-affiliated commit on ₣BO. The restoration is operational cleanup that any session would benefit from, not bench-specific work — naming it as ₣BO-level housekeeping rather than pace-internal keeps the bench pace's commit history focused on actual measurement work.

Want me to proceed with option 1?

input.content:
# jjezs_reslate BOAAA

## Character

Measurement pace. Hand-orchestrated; goal is a defensible, replication-
backed conclusion plus a memo recommendation. Solidity over speed —
cost is acceptable, weak data is not.

## Motivation

`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32` is currently in effect on the
canest depot (canest2bhl100011 carries a 96-core quota approval).
The original A/B benchmark in ₣BL's stabled pace ₢BLAAA called for
two depot lifecycles; that structural path is blocked because the
existing quota cannot be re-levied at default 2-cpu without
forfeiting it. This pace measures the current depot's per-build
substep durations against historic clean-gauntlet baselines whose
depots ran at `e2-standard-2`. The structural finding from ₢BLAAA
(machineType is pool-time, not build-time) carries over and earns
the memo its keep regardless of measurement noise.

## Pre-conditions

Before any bench operation:
  1. Verify the depot is alive via `tt/rbw-dl.PayorListsDepots.sh`.
  2. Verify `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32` is still set in
     `.rbk/rbrr.env`, alongside cancbhl-/canrbhl- prefixes and
     RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=canest2bhl100011.

(Operational state was restored to main as heat-housekeeping before
this pace mounted; the verification above is a sanity check, not a
restoration step. A future pristine-lifecycle fixture run may
overwrite rbrr.env again — if so, recover from commit history.)

## What — three Cloud Build workloads, single-case run via tabtargets

Replication targets are 1 warmup + N data per workload; warmup runs
are discarded (worker-pool cold-cache, registry-cache cold).

1. moriah bottle ordain (`rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`), 1 warmup + 3 data
   = 4 runs. Airgap nameplate — ordain triggers forge→airgap chain
   (multi-build); wall-clock cost is correspondingly higher than a
   single conjure but exercises both the tether pool (forge) and
   airgap pool (airgap conjure) in one workload.
2. plantuml bind (`rbev-bottle-plantuml`), 1 warmup + 2 data = 3 runs.
   Multi-arch + SBOM upper bound, runs on tether pool.
3. Vouch, 1 warmup + 2 data = 3 runs. Scope each vouch to a single
   hallmark rather than letting `rbw-fV` sweep all hallmarks — the
   sweep's wall-clock conflates per-hallmark cost. Use moriah and
   plantuml hallmarks produced above as the vouch targets, rotating.
   Vouch is hardwired to the airgap pool regardless of vessel mode.

Skip reliquary inscribe and enshrine — registry-I/O dominated, won't
move with CPU. Yesterday's levy already exercised one inscribe and
one busybox conjure on this depot; their durations fold into the
memo as side data.

## How — data capture

For every Cloud Build triggered (including warmup discards):
  - Capture `gcloud builds describe BUILD_ID --format=json` — the
    full record preserves per-step `startTime`/`endTime`,
    `options.machineType`, queue time, pool routing, and step
    `status`. This is the substrate the memo builds on.
  - Capture the tabtarget's host log alongside (orchestration
    overhead is secondary signal but trivial to keep).
  - Per-build artifacts live under `Memos/bench-data-YYYYMMDD/`
    as `<workload>-run-N.json` plus a manifest noting build ID,
    timestamp, expected vs actual machineType, pool, and any
    anomalies. The memo references the directory; raw data
    survives independently of the memo's analysis choices.

If a build fails mid-bench: capture the failed build's JSON anyway
(failure mode is data) and replace that run rather than silently
retrying. The memo must be honest about run-count.

## How — baseline mining

Four historic clean-pass runs are available, all on e2-standard-2:

| Date | Host | Fixtures | Log |
|------|------|----------|-----|
| May 14 | cerebro | 12 | `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260514-203134-1368806-985.txt` |
| May 12 | cerebro | 11 | `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260512-201330-1167187-166.txt` |
| May 12 | mac local | 11 | `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260512-125848-3104-247.txt` |
| May 6 | mac local | 11 | `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260506-110454-14392-84.txt` |

May 14 cerebro is the canonical reference (matches today's 12-fixture
shape including dockerfile-hygiene). The other three are
cross-checks. Pull the build IDs each gauntlet run produced for the
three target workloads (moriah airgap chain, plantuml bind, vouch)
from each log, then `gcloud builds describe` them — the records are
retained for 30+ days. Variance across the four historic runs sets
the noise floor against which today's highcpu deltas are judged.

## Outcome

Memo at `Memos/memo-YYYYMMDD-cloudbuild-machinetype-bench.md`
carrying:
  - The structural finding from ₢BLAAA (machineType is pool-time,
    not build-time; consequence for any future tuning effort).
  - Per-workload comparison table — 32cpu (today, data runs only)
    vs 2cpu (four historic baselines). Decompose by GCB step where
    the build's `steps[]` permits; show mean and spread across
    replications on both sides.
  - Per-step ratios alongside absolute deltas. Ratios are robust to
    queue-time and registry-cache variance; deltas tell the
    operational story.
  - Noise-floor section: across the four historic 2cpu baselines,
    what is the natural step-by-step variance? Today's deltas must
    clear that floor to count as signal.
  - Verdict per workload, not summed: CPU-bound (proportional
    speedup), IO-bound (no movement), or mixed (which step types
    move). The same machineType bump may yield different verdicts
    for forge vs airgap conjure vs bind vs vouch.
  - Recommendation: keep `e2-highcpu-32` as default, roll back to
    `e2-standard-2`, or pick a middle value. The recommendation
    must cite per-workload numbers, not gestalt.

## Constraints

  - No production-config changes from this pace. Rollback (or non-
    rollback) lives in the cantle pace.
  - GCB substep durations are host-independent — server-recorded by
    GCB, retrieved by `gcloud builds describe`. Tabtarget wall-clock
    totals carry host-dependent orchestration overhead (git ops,
    gcloud RPCs, polling); the memo distinguishes the two.
  - 96-core quota stays intact — no depot levy/unmake operations.

toolUseResult.content:
# jjezs_reslate BOAAA

## Character

Measurement pace. Hand-orchestrated; goal is a defensible, replication-
backed conclusion plus a memo recommendation. Solidity over speed —
cost is acceptable, weak data is not.

## Motivation

`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32` is currently in effect on the
canest depot (canest2bhl100011 carries a 96-core quota approval).
The original A/B benchmark in ₣BL's stabled pace ₢BLAAA called for
two depot lifecycles; that structural path is blocked because the
existing quota cannot be re-levied at default 2-cpu without
forfeiting it. This pace measures the current depot's per-build
substep durations against historic clean-gauntlet baselines whose
depots ran at `e2-standard-2`. The structural finding from ₢BLAAA
(machineType is pool-time, not build-time) carries over and earns
the memo its keep regardless of measurement noise.

## Pre-conditions

Before any bench operation:
  1. Verify the depot is alive via `tt/rbw-dl.PayorListsDepots.sh`.
  2. Verify `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32` is still set in
     `.rbk/rbrr.env`, alongside cancbhl-/canrbhl- prefixes and
     RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=canest2bhl100011.

(Operational state was restored to main as heat-housekeeping before
this pace mounted; the verification above is a sanity check, not a
restoration step. A future pristine-lifecycle fixture run may
overwrite rbrr.env again — if so, recover from commit history.)

## What — three Cloud Build workloads, single-case run via tabtargets

Replication targets are 1 warmup + N data per workload; warmup runs
are discarded (worker-pool cold-cache, registry-cache cold).

1. moriah bottle ordain (`rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap`), 1 warmup + 3 data
   = 4 runs. Airgap nameplate — ordain triggers forge→airgap chain
   (multi-build); wall-clock cost is correspondingly higher than a
   single conjure but exercises both the tether pool (forge) and
   airgap pool (airgap conjure) in one workload.
2. plantuml bind (`rbev-bottle-plantuml`), 1 warmup + 2 data = 3 runs.
   Multi-arch + SBOM upper bound, runs on tether pool.
3. Vouch, 1 warmup + 2 data = 3 runs. Scope each vouch to a single
   hallmark rather than letting `rbw-fV` sweep all hallmarks — the
   sweep's wall-clock conflates per-hallmark cost. Use moriah and
   plantuml hallmarks produced above as the vouch targets, rotating.
   Vouch is hardwired to the airgap pool regardless of vessel mode.

Skip reliquary inscribe and enshrine — registry-I/O dominated, won't
move with CPU. Yesterday's levy already exercised one inscribe and
one busybox conjure on this depot; their durations fold into the
memo as side data.

## How — data capture

For every Cloud Build triggered (including warmup discards):
  - Capture `gcloud builds describe BUILD_ID --format=json` — the
    full record preserves per-step `startTime`/`endTime`,
    `options.machineType`, queue time, pool routing, and step
    `status`. This is the substrate the memo builds on.
  - Capture the tabtarget's host log alongside (orchestration
    overhead is secondary signal but trivial to keep).
  - Per-build artifacts live under `Memos/bench-data-YYYYMMDD/`
    as `<workload>-run-N.json` plus a manifest noting build ID,
    timestamp, expected vs actual machineType, pool, and any
    anomalies. The memo references the directory; raw data
    survives independently of the memo's analysis choices.

If a build fails mid-bench: capture the failed build's JSON anyway
(failure mode is data) and replace that run rather than silently
retrying. The memo must be honest about run-count.

## How — baseline mining

Four historic clean-pass runs are available, all on e2-standard-2:

| Date | Host | Fixtures | Log |
|------|------|----------|-----|
| May 14 | cerebro | 12 | `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260514-203134-1368806-985.txt` |
| May 12 | cerebro | 11 | `hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260512-201330-1167187-166.txt` |
| May 12 | mac local | 11 | `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260512-125848-3104-247.txt` |
| May 6 | mac local | 11 | `../logs-buk/hist-rbw-tP-sh-20260506-110454-14392-84.txt` |

May 14 cerebro is the canonical reference (matches today's 12-fixture
shape including dockerfile-hygiene). The other three are
cross-checks. Pull the build IDs each gauntlet run produced for the
three target workloads (moriah airgap chain, plantuml bind, vouch)
from each log, then `gcloud builds describe` them — the records are
retained for 30+ days. Variance across the four historic runs sets
the noise floor against which today's highcpu deltas are judged.

## Outcome

  - Noise-floor section: across the four historic 2cpu baselines,
